data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Investors have two major misjudgments regarding the situation in Iran.
data.description: The U.S. economy is not as immune to the energy shocks caused by war as many investors believe. Michael Cembalest, Chairman of Markets and Investment Strategy at JPMorgan Asset and Wealth Management, pointed out in a new report released this Monday that two widely accepted but fundamentally flawed assessments circulate in the market regarding the conflict with Iran: First, the U.S. economy can withstand the impact of a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz due to its energy independence; second, the pressure from Iran reopening the strait is enough to force a rapid de-escalation of the situation. Cembalest believes both assessments are overly optimistic. The report was released as Trump's latest deadline for Iran to immediately reopen the Strait of Hormuz expires Tuesday evening. Meanwhile, the decline in the US stock market during this conflict has been relatively limited, which some investors interpret as a signal of market "immunity" to the situation. However, Cembalest's analysis suggests that this calm may be based on a systemic underestimation of risk. Misconception 1: US Energy Independence Can Withstand External Shocks. Cembalest directly addresses this market consensus in his report: "The claim that the US is immune to the impact of a Strait of Hormuz blockade is fundamentally wrong. US fossil fuel independence does not constitute an economic firewall as you might imagine." This conclusion is supported not by theoretical deduction, but by the actual market movements. While the outside world generally focuses on the risks faced by European and Asian countries due to the Strait of Hormuz blockade, the reality is that the price increases of various refined petroleum products, and even crude oil itself, have been more significant in the US market. This means that even though the United States is a net exporter of certain fuels, a sharp rise in global energy prices will still be transmitted to the US domestic market through market mechanisms, having a substantial impact on consumers and businesses. Misconception Two: Iran Will Be Forced to Make Quick Concessions. The second misconception is that some market participants believe that US military pressure and economic costs will force Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz as soon as possible. Cembalest holds reservations about this. In his report, he cites the views of Bloomberg Middle East economist Dina Esfandiary, who points out that Iran has realized that the strategy of holding the global economy hostage is less costly and more effective than expected. In other words, Iran has concluded from the current situation that this strategy has unexpectedly worked. Cembalest also lists several structural factors that make it difficult for the situation to end quickly. First, even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens tomorrow, oil production in the region will take time to recover to pre-conflict levels. Second, the interceptor missile stockpiles of the United States, Israel, and Gulf states may be running low. Furthermore, Iran's significant progress in drone manufacturing has greatly enhanced its ability to conduct asymmetric warfare. Cembalest wrote in his report, "While drones have small payloads, they can inflict enormous damage on much more expensive aircraft, ships, and radar systems with just a small payload, and the payload per unit cost of a drone is higher than that of many missile systems." The US Navy's minesweeping capabilities are also a concern—currently, the fleet has only four aging minesweepers, all of which are scheduled for retirement. Despite the accumulating risks mentioned above, the US stock market has remained relatively stable in this conflict, with a significantly smaller decline than the historic shocks of last year's tariff war, the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict, and the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic. In an interview with MarketWatch, Stephanie Link, chief investment strategist at Hightower Advisors, said the resilience of U.S. stocks was "fascinating," attributing it to two main factors: upward revisions to earnings forecasts by Wall Street analysts and a robust U.S. labor market. However, Link also cautioned about tail risks: "If the conflict lasts longer than a few months, I think the impact on the market and the U.S. economy will certainly be more severe." Cembalest, in the opening of his report, used Stephen King's novel *Salem's Town* as a metaphor, suggesting that the current situation may unfold very differently from initial expectations—the protagonist goes to fight evil with good intentions, only to have the town razed to the ground and everyone's situation worsened. This metaphor may be his most concise assessment of the entire situation in Iran.

data.title: Stablecoins are killing SWIFT. Someone has already laid the pipeline.
data.description: Stablecoins are eating into cross-border payments, and a Hong Kong-listed company has already laid the groundwork. In 2025, the global stablecoin market capitalization exceeded $230 billion, with on-chain transaction volume surpassing traditional payment giant PayPal. But the truly noteworthy change is not on-chain, but off-chain: Stablecoins are transforming from an internal tool for crypto transactions into the infrastructure for cross-border payments for businesses. This transformation is happening faster than most people imagine. OSL Group (863.HK), a digital asset platform listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange's main board, saw its core operating revenue increase by 150.1% year-on-year in 2025, reaching HK$534 million. The platform's total transaction volume exceeded HK$201.2 billion, more than doubling year-on-year. But the most crucial figures are hidden in the structure: Stablecoin trading volume accounts for 60% of the platform's trading-related volume, and overseas revenue accounts for 67%. In other words, more than half of this company's business is no longer about helping people speculate on Bitcoin, but about helping businesses move money using stablecoins. This isn't just the story of an exchange. This is a company that is rebuilding global capital flow channels using compliance licenses and stablecoin payments. Stablecoin payments: not transfers, but rebuilding channels. Stablecoins have been in the crypto market for seven or eight years, and for most of that time they've been doing the same thing: acting as a lubricant between trading pairs. You sell ETH, receive USDT, and then buy BTC. It's the base currency for exchanges, a substitute for the US dollar in the on-chain world. But starting in 2024, things began to change. More and more businesses found that stablecoins were far more efficient than traditional banking channels for payments and cross-border settlements. The reason isn't complicated: a cross-border remittance through the SWIFT system takes an average of 1 to 5 business days, has high fees, and incomplete coverage, especially in emerging markets. A payment from Hong Kong to Nigeria might go through three or four intermediary banks, each adding its own markup. The advantage of stablecoins is straightforward: 24/7 operation, funds arrive in minutes, and costs are almost negligible. For SMEs that handle large amounts of cross-border payments every quarter, this isn't just technological hype; it's real money saving. The problem lies in the significant gap between "transferring USDT on-chain" and "a company actually completing a compliant cross-border payment." You need a compliant stablecoin, fiat currency deposit and withdrawal channels, access to local payment networks, multi-currency exchange capabilities, and most importantly—a license recognized by regulatory agencies. This is precisely what OSL is doing. In February 2026, OSL launched USDGO, an enterprise-grade USD stablecoin issued by Anchorage Digital, the first US-based crypto bank to receive a federally licensed certificate. It is regulated by the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) and its reserves are primarily US Treasury bonds and cash. This is not just another copycat move of "I want to issue a stablecoin too." USDGO follows a path of issuance under US federal regulation and compliant distribution in Asia, with a clear goal: to provide enterprise clients with a stablecoin they can confidently use. But simply having a coin isn't enough. OSL BizPay is a one-stop stablecoin payment solution for businesses, allowing merchants to accept crypto payments and settle directly in local fiat currency. StableHub serves as the central hub for stablecoin exchange, supporting high-speed conversions between different stablecoins and fiat currencies. Furthermore, with the acquisition of Banxa in January 2026, a Web3 payment infrastructure provider with extensive payment licenses in North America, Europe, the UK, and Australia, OSL has completed the final mile of fiat currency access. In short, OSL isn't just building a product, but a complete pipeline from on-chain to bank accounts. USDGO is the water in the pipeline, BizPay is the tap for enterprises, StableHub is the central dispatch station, and Banxa is the interface connecting to the global banking system. This also explains why payment business will become the core engine of OSL's annual growth in 2025. The 60% share of stablecoin trading volume is not accidental, but a signal that this pipeline system is starting to work. Why do institutions choose OSL: Compliance is the most expensive ticket? Some may ask: OSL is not the only stablecoin payment platform, so why do institutions choose it? The answer is not because OSL has lower fees, nor because it lists more cryptocurrencies. On the contrary, OSL currently only supports BTC and ETH trading on the retail side, demonstrating extreme restraint. The real reason institutions choose OSL can be summed up in two words: compliance. In December 2020, OSL became the first digital asset platform in Hong Kong to obtain Type 1 and Type 7 licenses from the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC). In 2023, after Hong Kong implemented a new regulatory framework for virtual asset trading platforms, OSL also obtained an AMLO VASP license, becoming one of the first platforms authorized to provide services to retail investors. As of early 2026, the group has obtained more than 50 licenses and registrations in more than 11 jurisdictions worldwide. The value of these licenses lies not on paper, but in the operational details. According to SFC regulations, OSL must store 98% of its client assets in cold wallets, with hot wallets accounting for no more than 2%. Data from 2024 shows that OSL's custody solution in Hong Kong has an insurance coverage of up to US$1 billion. More importantly, client assets are held in trust through a wholly-owned subsidiary, legally completely separated from the platform's own assets. Even if the platform goes bankrupt and is liquidated, client assets are not subject to liquidation. For institutions managing billions of dollars, these are not advantages, but bottom lines. This is why OSL secured approximately 64% of the custody and trading share when Hong Kong's virtual asset spot ETFs were listed. When traditional mutual funds like Bosera and China Asset Management choose digital asset custodians, their primary consideration is always "Is it safe to keep the money with you?" OSL answered this question with SOC 2 Type 2 certification, a bankruptcy remote trust, and a $1 billion insurance coverage. In 2025, OSL further became the sole custodian and trading platform for Asia's first Solana spot ETF, and in the same year, it also distributed Asia's first retail tokenized fund—the Huaxia Hong Kong Dollar Digital Currency Market Fund. Also in 2025, OSL completed a $300 million equity financing, setting a record at the time for the largest equity financing in the Asian digital asset industry outside of India and Japan; in 2026, the company completed another $200 million equity financing. These two capital market moves not only provided ample ammunition for the company to expand its international compliant payment business, but also further enriched the company's strategic long-term shareholder base, demonstrating the favor of institutional investors. Compliance is not a cost, but a moat. In an industry where even large offshore exchanges can have their assets frozen overnight, OSL's 50+ licenses are all barriers built with time and money. Newcomers wanting to replicate this model will face years just for the license application process. OSL's executives also possess extensive industry experience; their resumes include experience at Google, Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, and numerous digital asset platforms and projects, giving the company a unique advantage of combining Web3 expertise with traditional financial knowledge. OSL's current transformation and repositioning as a "global stablecoin payment and trading platform" is not just marketing rhetoric, but a tangible revenue structure reflected in its 2025 financial report. The Other Side of the Coin Of course, making the OSL story too smooth would be unrealistic. In fiscal year 2025, OSL suffered a net loss of HK$388 million. The same period of the previous year saw a profit of HK$54.8 million. Numerically, this is a rather striking reversal. However, breaking down the loss reveals that it primarily consisted of: human and IT investments in global expansion, integration costs from the Banxa acquisition, license application fees in multiple jurisdictions, and fluctuations in the fair value of its digital assets. Many of these were one-off expenses. This wasn't a passive loss, but rather a proactive choice made in line with the macroeconomic cycle. While revenue hit a record high, losses widened, a phenomenon not uncommon for high-growth tech companies. The real question is: can these investments translate into economies of scale? The market seems to be giving initial positive signals. Total revenue in 2025 reached HK$489 million, a year-on-year increase of 30.4%. Core operating income reached HK$534 million, an increase of 150.1%. Platform transaction volume reached HK$201.2 billion, more than doubling. Overseas revenue jumped from almost zero to 67%. These figures indicate that the expansion funds were indeed spent on areas that generated revenue. It is worth noting that OSL uses the net method for revenue recognition, recording only the actual price difference and commission earned, rather than counting transaction volume as revenue. This means its HK$489 million revenue is of higher quality than some of its peers calculated using the gross method. In Hong Kong, OSL's most direct competitor was HashKey. The difference in their approaches is quite interesting: OSL follows the "compliant infrastructure provider" route, making money through licenses, payment networks, and institutional services, with a low-key and restrained style. HashKey, on the other hand, is more like a "traffic ecosystem" model, issuing its ecosystem token HSK, building a public blockchain, and engaging in frequent community interaction. In 2025, HashKey's total transaction volume reached HK$590.8 billion, but its net loss during the same period reached HK$1.087 billion. Currently, the two are beginning to decouple. HashKey continues to focus on on-chain native finance, based in Hong Kong, and betting on the crypto ecosystem, but is expected to face the dual challenges of market volatility and competition from peers. OSL, on the other hand, has chosen to fully commit to internationalization, betting on a compliant stablecoin ecosystem, using both payments and transactions as its two pillars, connecting Web2 and Web3, hoping to mitigate the impact of market volatility. However, its growth potential will be affected by international regulatory policies and the implementation of the stablecoin ecosystem. It's too early to conclude which path will prevail. But one thing is certain: in the stablecoin payment sector, institutional clients' decisions currently indicate a preference for compliance. Summary The competition in stablecoin payments has only just begun. The global regulatory framework is still taking shape, and corporate demand is still being released. Whoever can simultaneously excel in compliance, product development, and global network development will hold the next big advantage. OSL, at least, is already at the table. Pipelines aren't sexy. But every transaction has to go through a pipeline.

data.title: The first batch of projects to perish in the bear market: Why did these 21 projects fail to survive?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 3, 2026, DeFi analyst Scribbler posted that 21 crypto projects announced closures or downsizing in Q1 of this year due to the bear market. Many believe that continued losses, insufficient liquidity, and industry iteration are the decisive factors for this wave of closures in the crypto field.

Cryptocurrency projects are experiencing a large-scale wave of closures, and the continued bear market has exposed the vulnerabilities of DeFi, NFTs, wallets, and games.

I. Why is the wave of closures so fierce?

Ultimately, this is a reshuffling driven by liquidity, and the closure of these projects is a sign of deleveraging. Since the crypto market crash last year, open interest has decreased by more than 40%. Projects lacking clear profit models or user retention rates have been forced to exit the market, making room for more resilient projects that have withstood the test.

Specifically, Bitcoin's approximately 35% drop from its October high triggered a chain reaction, ultimately resulting in the liquidation of $19 billion in assets within 24 hours. This was the largest single-day deleveraging event in cryptocurrency history, directly draining funds from the market and making it impossible for projects reliant on speculative trading to sustain operations. In the fourth quarter of last year alone, open interest on exchanges fell by more than 40%, indicating a significant reduction in leveraged capital. The market shifted from aggressive speculation to risk aversion, with the derivatives-to-spot ratio remaining high at 9.6. This suggests that traders were hedging or shorting rather than establishing long-term positions, further exacerbating the liquidity crunch. Fund flow data shows the market is shrinking. While stablecoins accounted for a record 75% of total cryptocurrency trading volume, user behavior presented a starkly different picture. Retail trading volume fell by 16% in the first quarter, marking the largest quarterly drop ever. This indicates that funds were concentrated in algorithmic trading and institutional flows, leaving smaller, retail-dependent projects lacking sufficient user base and trading volume to cover fixed costs. A vicious cycle triggered by capital flows has created a liquidity crisis. Meanwhile, capital has shifted to Bitcoin ETFs and large assets, making it difficult for smaller projects to survive. This wave of shutdowns has also made projects realize that they should no longer engage in hype or rapid expansion, but rather focus on survival and sustainable development. Projects created based on incentives and short-term stimulus are gradually disappearing, while projects with real practical applications are emerging. This change may quietly lay the foundation for a more stable and mature phase of blockchain project development in the future. II. Detailed Overview of 21 Projects in the Shutdown Wave 1. Fantasy Top Creators and Time: Fantasy Top was created on May 1, 2024, jointly created by Travis Bickle (anonymous) and Kipit. **Basic Information:** Fantasy Top is a SocialFi collectible card game (TCG) based on Blast L2, where users can trade NFT-style "hero" cards from crypto KOLs and participate in prediction contests. The project initially aimed to become the financial infrastructure for monetizing creator content. Version V2 is now live, completing core functions such as card trading, prediction, and staking. It was once among the top ten protocols in DeFiLlama in terms of fees and revenue. In March 2026, it announced that the Core mode would be shut down in mid-June. **Funding Overview:** Fantasy Top completed a Pre-Seed round in April 2024 (amount undisclosed); in December of the same year, it completed a $4.25 million seed round, led by Dragonfly Capital. It experienced explosive growth in its early stages, relying heavily on speculative user support and lacking stable revenue. It has not achieved sustained profitability, with funds primarily used for product iteration. Reasons for failure: Lack of genuine user demand; it was an attention mining project. 2. Magic Eden (ME Wallet) Creators and timeline: ME Wallet was launched in 2024 by Jack Lu, Zhuoxun Yin, Sidney Zhang, and Zhuojie Zhou (former executives of dYdX and Coinbase). Basic information: Magic Eden is a leading NFT marketplace in the Solana ecosystem, supporting multi-chain NFT transactions and Launchpad. ME Wallet is its cross-chain self-custodied wallet, focusing on NFT asset management. The wallet already had relatively complete functions, supporting iOS/Android, hardware wallet integration, and 2FA. It was developed by the Exodus team and audited by Halborn. On April 1, 2026, it was announced that ME Wallet entered "export-only mode," and on May 1, it completely ceased operations. **Funding Overview:** In March 2022, Magic Eden completed a $27 million Series A funding round (led by Sequoia Capital); in June 2022, it raised $130 million in Series B funding, valuing the company at $1.6 billion. Magic Eden profits from transaction fees, but its wallets are primarily unprofitable. **Reasons for Failure:** The wallet market is dominated by star projects like MetaMask, making it difficult for new dark horses to emerge. The closure of ME Wallet signifies the failure of Magic Eden's wallet product. **3. Leap Wallet** **Creator and Timeline:** Founded in November 2021 by Sanjeev Rao. **Basic Information:** Leap Wallet is the flagship multi-chain wallet of the Cosmos ecosystem, supporting 200+ public chains (Cosmos/EVM/Bitcoin). It offers mobile, browser plugin, and web-based (Leapboard) wallets, focusing on staking, cross-chain swap, and airdrop management. It is one of the wallets with the largest user base in the Cosmos ecosystem, boasting extremely high ecosystem integration. On April 3, 2026, Leap Wallet announced that it would cease operations on May 28, requiring users to migrate as soon as possible. The discontinuation also includes Compass Wallet (extension, iOS, Android), Leap WebApp, Swapfast, Leap Cosmos Hub validator nodes, and Leap Cosmos Snaps. **Funding Review:** In April 2022, Leap Wallet raised $3.2 million in seed funding, valuing the project at $40 million at the time. Reasons for failure: No stable profit model, high operating costs. 4. Dmail (Dmail Network) Creators and timeline: Dmail was founded in 2021 by James Wen and Daniel James. Basic information: Dmail provides decentralized email services, combining Web3 domains (DNS), NFT emails, and on-chain push notifications. It has over 4.05 million on-chain domain accounts and processes over 90 million messages. On April 3, 2026, it announced that it would gradually cease operations starting May 15. Users need to export their email data and cancel their accounts. **Funding Review:** On November 30, 2021, a seed round of financing was conducted, raising $10 million; on February 9, 2022, a strategic round of financing was conducted, with the specific amount undisclosed; on July 24, 2023, a Pre-A round of financing was conducted, with the specific amount undisclosed, and the project valuation at that time was $60 million. **Reasons for Failure:** Users are accustomed to traditional email, and decentralized email is currently a non-proposition. **5. Intergaze** **Creator and Time:** Created in 2025. **Basic Information:** Intergaze is a zero-gas-fee public chain optimized for NFTs, based on Cosmos, supporting cross-chain asset transfers, and focusing on low-cost NFT minting and trading. The mainnet is already online, and the ecosystem is initially built, but there are few users and NFT projects, and insufficient liquidity. On April 3, 2026, Intergaze announced it would gradually cease operations, and users must withdraw their assets within 14 days before the cross-chain shutdown (before April 16). NFT holders' NFTs will be migrated to Stargaze on CosmosHub in the second half of May. Users must register a Cosmos wallet before May 1 to be included in the migration. Funding Review: No publicly disclosed fundraising records. Reason for Failure: The NFT market cooled down. 6. Yupp AI Creator and Time: Yupp AI launched in June 2025, created by former Coinbase VP Pankaj Gupta. Basic Information: Yupp AI is a crowdsourced AI model evaluation platform, positioned as the "Yelp" of the AI ​​era. Users compared 800+ models, including ChatGPT and Claude, and submitted feedback to earn token incentives; the platform also provided RLHF data to large model companies. Within a year of its launch, it had 1.3 million registered users and collected millions of feedback monthly, but had zero paying users. It announced its closure on March 31, 2026, returning remaining funds to investors, and retaining the website for 15 days for data download. Funding Review: Completed a $33 million seed round in 2025, led by a16z Crypto. Reasons for Failure: No one paid for the service, and the competition in the AI ​​field was extremely fierce. 7. Tally (Tally.xyz) Creators and Time: Founded in 2019 by Dennison Bertram and Rafael Solari. Basic Information: Tally was once a top-tier DAO on-chain governance platform, supporting proposals, voting, and treasury management for thousands of DAOs, including Uniswap, AAVE, and Arbitrum. It processed over $1 billion in payments and managed over $80 billion in assets. 2026 Funding Review: Tally had enterprise subscription revenue, but it wasn't enough to cover costs. On April 22, 2025, it completed an $8 million Series A funding round. On February 4, 2026, it announced an ICO and planned to publicly document the entire process over the next 60 days. On March 17, Tally announced its closure. Reason for Failure: DAO governance was no longer a necessary option. 8. Fey Protocol Creator and Time: Fey Protocol was created in 2024. Basic Information: Fey Protocol is a permissionless token launchpad based on the Base chain, allowing anyone to issue tokens without approval. Revenue is automatically distributed to FEY stakers. It supports token creation, liquidity pools, and trading. It announced its cessation of operations in February 2026. The team disbanded, and users had to redeem their assets. Funding Overview: No public fundraising. Relies on transaction fees. Reasons for Failure: Intense competition and a proliferation of low-quality projects. 9. Angle Protocol Creator and Time: Angle Protocol was created by Pablo Veyrat in 2021. **Basic Information:** Angle Protocol is a decentralized stablecoin protocol that issues the Euro stablecoin EURA and the US dollar stablecoin USDA. It was once the largest algorithmic stablecoin for the Euro, with a peak circulating supply of 180 million. In March 2026, the community voted to shut it down. Operations ceased on March 1, 2027. Users must redeem their stablecoins through the Angle application on Ethereum before March 1, 2027, at a 1:1 exchange rate with no loss. After the redemption period ends, unredeemed reserves will be airdropped to EURA and USDA holders on Ethereum via Merkl, with an additional one-year claim period, for a total of two years of redemption window. The Angle team has transitioned to Merkl, focusing on a DeFi incentive platform. **Funding Overview:** In 2021, it raised $5 million in seed funding, led by a16z, valuing the company at $50 million.

Reasons for Failure:Intense competition in the stablecoin market, long-term business losses, and severe user churn.

10. DataHaven.xyz

Creator and Time:Created at the end of 2024.

Basic Information:DataHaven.xyz is a decentralized storage protocol, specifically designed to provide on-chain data storage for AI Agents and DeFi. Based on Moonbeam, it combines EigenLayer for restaking security. It launched the CampHaven.xyz airdrop program, but did not reach the stage of full commercial use on the mainnet. It announced its closure on March 15, 2026.

Funding Review:No public fundraising.

Reasons for Failure:Failure to find a sustainable development path.

11. Step Finance Creator and Time: Step Finance launched its mainnet in December 2020. Basic Information: Step Finance is a leading DeFi dashboard in the Solana ecosystem, providing one-stop asset management, trading, staking, and NFT analytics. It has over 350,000 monthly active users. On February 24, 2026, Step Finance officially announced that Step Finance, SolanaFloor, and Remora Markets would cease all business operations. Funding Review: $2 million was raised in a private placement in April 2021. Reason for Failure: A hacker attack occurred at the end of January, resulting in the theft of approximately $40 million. 12. Parsec Finance Creator and Time: Founded in 2020 by Will Sheehan. Basic Information: Parsec Finance was a professional on-chain data analytics platform providing institutions and whales with real-time data, charts, and trading signals for DeFi/NFT/Bitcoin Ordinals. It supported 13 EVM chains. It was a top-tier data tool in the industry, with a comprehensive API and Enterprise Edition (Team). On February 20, 2026, Parsec announced the cessation of its services. Funding Overview: $1.25 million in seed funding in 2021 (led by Polychain); $4 million in Series A funding in 2023 (led by Galaxy Digital). Reason for Failure: No paying users. 13. ZeroLend Creator and Time: Created by Ryker in January 2023. Basic Information: ZeroLend is a multi-chain decentralized lending protocol focusing on L2 platforms like zkSync and Manta. It supports lending of liquid restaking tokens, RWA, and BTCFi assets. It was once the largest lending protocol on zkSync. Its peak TVL was $359 million (November 2024), which then plummeted 98% to $6.6 million. On February 17, 2026, it announced the cessation of operations. Funding Review: It raised $3 million in its seed round in 2024, valuing the company at $25 million. Profitability was poor, lending spreads were thin, and security costs were high. Reason for failure: Continuous losses. 14. PolynomialFi Creator and timeline: PolynomialFi was founded in 2021. Basic information: PolynomialFi is an on-chain options and perpetual contract liquidity aggregation protocol that improves derivatives capital efficiency. It has accumulated over $4.7 billion in trading volume and serves over 100,000 users. Trade 2.0 was launched in August 2024, demonstrating mature technology. An orderly shutdown was announced on February 15, 2026. Funding review: Seed round financing was completed in 2023. Reason for failure: Correct direction, but execution failed. 15. Nifty Gateway Creators and Timeline: Founded in 2018 by brothers Duncan & Griffin Cock Foster. Basic Information: Nifty Gateway was an early top-tier NFT art platform, acquired by Gemini (Winklevoss brothers) in 2019. It primarily focused on compliant fiat currency (credit card) purchases and partnered with Beeple, Eminem, The Weeknd, and others. It reached its peak during the NFT bubble, with monthly sales reaching hundreds of millions of dollars. After the market crash, it transformed into Nifty Gateway Studio (brand collaboration) in 2024. On January 25, 2026, it announced its official closure on February 23, and entered a "cash-out only" mode, giving users one month to transfer their NFT assets and funds. Financial Status: Fully acquired by Gemini, it had ample funds. However, after the NFT market cooled down, it continued to suffer losses.

Reason for failure:The NFT market cooled down.

16. Slingshot Crypto (Slingshot Finance)

Founders and timeline:Founded in 2018, launched in 2020. Created by Clinton Bembry, Scott Lewis, and Zak Cole.

Basic information:Slingshot Crypto is a veteran DEX aggregator (formerly DEX.AG), providing cross-chain optimal price routing. Supports multi-chain swaps and limit orders. Acquired by Magic Eden in April 2025.

Funding review:Completed a seed round of $3.1 million in 2020; completed a Series A round of $15 million in 2021.

Reason for failure:Acquired by Magic Eden.

17. Runeverse Game (Forgotten Runeverse) Creator and Time: Created in the second half of 2022 by the NFT project Forgotten Runes Wizard’s Cult. Basic Information: Runeverse Game is a pixel-style MMORPG blockchain game based on the Ronin blockchain (Axie Infinity). It is free to play, features NFT characters/equipment, and supports PVE/PVP. It previously received development licenses for console platforms (Nintendo/PS/Xbox). It launched globally in 2025 and was fully operational before the servers shut down. On January 27, 2026, it announced an indefinite server shutdown. Funding Review: Received investment from Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian. Game revenue could not cover server and operating costs. Reasons for Failure: Continuous losses.

18.Sound.xyz (Soundxyz)

Creators and Time:Founded in 2021 by David Greenstein, Vignesh Hirudayakan, and Matt Mazur.

Basic Information:Sound.xyz was a Web3 music NFT platform that provided independent musicians with limited-edition music NFT releases and high royalty rates (far exceeding Spotify). Tools were made available to all artists in July 2023. It generated a total of $5.5 million in revenue for creators. It announced its closure in March 2026. Users need to withdraw their music NFTs and any unwithdrawn earnings.

Funding Review: $5 million in seed funding in 2021 (a16z, 21 Savage); $20 million in Series A funding in 2023, for a total of $25 million. Reason for failure: Continuous losses. 19. MilkyWay Creator and time: Created by JayB Kim in December 2023. Basic information: MilkyWay is the liquidity staking center for the Celestia modular ecosystem. It provides liquidity staking for TIA holders, issues receipt tokens milkTIA, and unlocks the liquidity of staked assets. It supports staking, unstake, and liquidity mining. On March 26, 2026, MilkyWay officially announced that it had officially started the L1 mainnet shutdown process. Before the shutdown, the team completed an on-chain upgrade and safely migrated all user assets from the MilkyWay L1 chain back to their respective native chains. Funding: $5 million seed round, led by Polychain, Binance Labs, and HackVC. Angel investors include Celestia co-founder Mustafa Al-Bassam. Reason for failure: Continuous losses. 20. Pixiland Social (Pixiland) Founders and timeline: Founded in 2023 by Ding Xiaochen and Shen Kun. Basic information: Pixiland Social was a Web3 image/video social platform where users could mint their content into NFTs and receive PIX tokens as incentives. It emphasized content ownership and the creator economy. It had hundreds of thousands of users and millions of posts. It announced its closure in March 2026. Users needed to withdraw their personal NFT content and PIX tokens. Funding review: Received strategic investment from KuCoin Ventures. Reason for failure: Continuous losses. 21. Blocto App Creator and Time: Created in 2020. Basic Information: Blocto App is a multi-chain smart contract wallet supporting Flow, Solana, Ethereum, Aptos, etc. It pioneered email/social account login, lowering the barrier to entry for Web3. It gained popularity due to the NBA Top Shot. It served over 2 million users, with widespread SDK integration and embedded Swap and cross-chain bridges. Wallet, Swap, and cross-chain services ceased on December 18, 2025. Users need to export their private keys/mnemonic phrases for migration. Funding Overview: Received two rounds of investment from Alameda Research, Animoca Brands, and Mark Cuban (owner of the NBA's Dallas Mavericks), with a valuation of $80 million in 2023. Reasons for Failure: FLOW's price plummeted by 99%, leading to ecosystem collapse. Conclusion: Countless crypto projects have risen during past crypto cycles, rapidly expanding through token incentives and liquidity advantages. However, when market hype dies down and liquidity dwindles, these projects lacking real-world application scenarios struggle to survive. As seen in the above cases, many projects had capital backing, technological backing, and even briefly topped their respective fields, but ultimately faded like shooting stars. Projects relying on short-term speculation were eliminated one by one by the market; only projects with genuine market fit and real-world applications could survive. Projects that took off in a bull market only won the first half; in the current bear market, only those who survive will have the capital to stand out from the crowd.

data.title: Argentine President Javier Milei Sold His People To The Libra Pump-Dump Scandal For A $5 Million Reward: New York Times Report
data.description:
Denials clash with emerging evidence

Argentine President Javier Milei is facing renewed scrutiny after new evidence had been found linking him to the Libra project, despite his repeated denial of having any connection to the project.

The controversy stems from Milei’s social post back in February 2025, where he seemingly promoted the Libra token as a tool to support Argentina’s economy by funding small businesses and startups.

🚨NEWS: New court documents reviewed by The New York Times reveal undisclosed calls and potential payments linking @JMilei, president of Argentina, to the $LIBRA token launch.

Phone logs and messages challenge Milei’s claim of no involvement, reigniting the crypto scandal as… pic.twitter.com/UKTKF820Jc

— SolanaFloor (@SolanaFloor) April 6, 2026

Milei endorsement briefly the token’s valuation shooting up before it collapsed by more than 96%, wiping out an estimated $251 million in investor funds.

Milei has consistently denied any involvement, stating that he was merely sharing a private initiative and had “no connection whatsoever” to the project.

"A few hours ago, I posted a tweet, like so many infinite other times, supporting an alleged private venture with which I obviously have no connection whatsoever."

Hace unas horas publiqué un tweet, como tantas otras infinitas veces, apoyando un supuesto emprendimiento privado del que obviamente no tengo vinculación alguna.

No estaba interiorizado de los pormenores del proyecto y luego de haberme interiorizado decidí no seguir dándole…

— Javier Milei (@JMilei) February 15, 2025

He added that he deleted the post after learning more about the token.

"I wasn't aware of the details of the project, and after becoming aware of them, I decided on to keep promoting it. That's why I deleted the tweet."

However, newly surfaced phone records reviewed by prosecutors and reported by The New York Times appear to challenge that narrative.

The logs show that Milei exchanged seven calls with an entrepreneur linked to the Libra project on the same night as his post, with communication occurring both before and after the promotion. While the contents of the calls remain unknown, the timing has raised fresh questions about the extent of his involvement.

Investigation widens as financial links emerge

The Libra collapse has triggered a broad legal and political response in Argentina. Federal prosecutors have opened an ongoing investigation, naming Milei as a person of interest, while lawyers have filed fraud complaints and some political figures have called for impeachment proceedings.

Additional findings have added further complexity to the case. Investigators reportedly uncovered a draft document on the phone of crypto lobbyist Mauricio Novelli—considered a key figure in the project—referencing a potential $5 million agreement tied to the promotion. The document, written just days before Milei’s post, does not specify recipients, leaving its purpose unclear.

A probe reveals President Javier Milei took $5M for the promotion of the Libra memecoin. 🚨

The file was recovered from a crypto lobbyist’s phone and describes a three stage payment structure linked to Milei publicly supporting the token.

Libra briefly reached $4B market cap… pic.twitter.com/5WE7AgvbtU

— Crypto Jargon (@Crypto_Jargon) March 15, 2026

Separately, reports indicate that wallets linked to insiders cashed out more than $100 million during the token’s rise and fall, intensifying concerns over possible coordinated activity.

Despite these developments, Argentina’s Anti-Corruption Office previously concluded that Milei did not violate public ethics rules, determining that his post was made in a personal capacity rather than as head of state.

The federal probe, however, remains active and continues to examine communications, financial links and the broader circumstances surrounding the token’s launch.

Political fallout meets crypto market risk

The Libra episode underscores the growing intersection between political influence and crypto markets, where a single high-profile endorsement can trigger rapid capital inflows—and equally rapid losses.

For Milei, the case represents a mounting test of credibility as new evidence continues to surface. While no direct proof has emerged linking the president to the token’s operations, the call records and financial clues have complicated his defense and extended the lifespan of the investigation.

$LIBRA crashing 94% was already bad.

But the bigger question now is whether political influence was tied to the trade from the start.

That’s what could keep this story alive longer than a normal memecoin scandal.#MileiLibra#BingXpic.twitter.com/n3rXiwMPEy

— Real DeFi (@Real_DeFi_) March 16, 2026

More broadly, the incident highlights the risks tied to politically amplified crypto projects, where market momentum can outpace transparency.

As regulators and investigators continue to examine the Libra collapse, the outcome may shape how public officials engage with digital assets—and how markets respond to their influence.

data.title: Bitget Wallet Expands QR Payment Feature Across Asia-Pacific to Include Multiple Blockchains
data.description:
Bitget Wallet Brings Crypto Payments to Everyday Shopping in Asia-Pacific

Bitget Wallet has rolled out QR-based crypto payments across the Asia-Pacific region, allowing users to make retail purchases directly from a self-custodial wallet.

By scanning local merchant QR codes, users can pay with stablecoins such as USDT and USDC across multiple blockchains, including Solana, BNB Chain, and Ethereum, while merchants continue to receive settlement through existing domestic payment systems.

BREAKING: We're the first crypto wallet to ship QR payments across Asia Pac 🌏

Pay with crypto directly:
> USDT/USDC → Pay with local currency
> No conversion headaches, just scan & pay

From now till Apr 22, get cashback with every successful QR payment.

Start scanning. pic.twitter.com/ro9RVLs5xP

— Bitget Wallet 🩵 (@BitgetWallet) April 2, 2026

How Are Crypto Payments Fitting into Daily Life

Alvin Kan, COO of Bitget Wallet, said,

“APAC is one of the clearest places where crypto payments can fit into existing consumer habits. QR payments are already part of daily life across the region. By connecting self-custodied assets and stablecoins to that payment experience, we are making crypto more practical for real-world use.”

The feature supports both local spending and cross-border travel, allowing users to pay in stores without converting to fiat or relying on traditional card networks.

The complete guide to using QR Pay:

1. Open app → Pay → QR Pay → Scan QR code
2. Enter the amount (local currency) + confirm

QR Pay is now live across popular regions in Asia Pacific. Try it now & get cashback.

— Bitget Wallet 🩵 (@BitgetWallet) April 2, 2026

Bitget Wallet plans to expand support beyond stablecoins to include over 1,000 tokens, covering mainstream coins, memetokens, and other digital assets.

Why APAC Is Leading the QR Payment Surge

Asia-Pacific has emerged as a key testing ground for QR-based digital payments.

According to Worldpay, digital wallets accounted for 59% of point-of-sale transaction value in APAC in 2024, compared with 38% globally.

The region also represented more than 60% of global QR payment activity in 2025.

Industry projections indicate that QR payment volumes worldwide will surpass $8 trillion by 2029, with Asia expected to remain the dominant market.

How Bitget Wallet Connects Crypto to Traditional Payments

The rollout is powered by Bitget Wallet’s Onchain Payments Matrix, an infrastructure layer linking blockchain networks, wallets, banks, and regional payment rails.

This system allows merchants to receive settlements through familiar domestic channels while supporting a wider adoption of crypto payments.

🚨LATEST: BITGET WALLET LAUNCHES ONCHAIN PAYMENTS MATRIX, INTEGRATING RIPPLE, MASTERCARD, VISA, AND MORE@BitgetWallet has unveiled the Onchain Payments Matrix (@PaymentsMatrix), a live stablecoin payment infrastructure connecting its 90 million users to 150 million+ merchants… pic.twitter.com/jGhoOd6swq

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 25, 2026

Alongside the expansion, Bitget Wallet has launched a QR code cashback campaign, offering users random token rewards for completing eligible transactions.

The initiative builds on the wallet’s earlier launches in markets like Vietnam, creating a region-wide network for crypto-enabled retail payments.

Can Crypto Become a Daily Payment Tool

By integrating with existing QR ecosystems, Bitget Wallet aims to tackle one of the major barriers in crypto adoption: real-world usability.

The strategy aligns with local consumer habits, enabling seamless payments for meals, groceries, transport, and travel-related purchases, all directly from self-custodial wallets.

APAC rollout is part of a wider plan to unify crypto payments across multiple channels, including QR codes, cards, and bank transfers, bringing self-custodial crypto closer to mainstream commerce.

data.title: De-casinoization: possibly the only right solution for the future of the crypto industry
data.description:
Author: Jtsong.eth; Source: X,@Jtsong2

The crypto industry has been talking about Mass Adoption, but in reality: users have always remained transaction users, not "users." AI has the potential to help blockchain achieve true "value accumulation."

Introduction: When Users Change from "Humans" to "AI"

If we look at the crypto industry over the past decade honestly enough, we will find a simple but often overlooked fact: crypto products have essentially always been "serving human nature."

This is also the core driving force behind the successive bull markets in cryptocurrencies. And what is human nature?

  • Profit-seeking

  • Gambling mentality

  • Leverage preference

  • Short-term feedback dependence

Therefore, the entire industry has naturally evolved the most successful model: Issuing Assets + Trading + PVP (Game Theory)

From ICOs to DeFi Summers, to NFTs, and to Memecoin, they all essentially revolve around:

  • “Can I buy it earlier?”

  • “Can I sell it faster?”

Shift:

  • Determinism

  • Verifiability

  • Trustless Execution

These three points are precisely the "essential requirements" of AI systems.

Why is this crucial?

Because AI systems themselves have a fundamental problem: the **black box**. The fact that model decisions are not interpretable becomes a systemic risk when it comes to: unexplainable model decisions, unverifiable data sources, unauditable reasoning processes, and the fact that these issues may be tolerable in the Web2 world. Blockchain offers a solution: **bringing the key processes of AI "on-chain."** This is not just a "bonus," but rather the essential infrastructure for the large-scale deployment of AI. An important conclusion: When the primary users of the crypto industry are still humans: playing with human nature is correct. But when the primary users begin to become AI: playing with determinism is the only solution. This is precisely why the next generation of Mass Adoption in the crypto industry is essentially a process of "de-casinoization." The prosperity of the casino model is essentially a short-term, effective structural benefit. We must acknowledge that the casino model is successful. It possesses several extremely strong advantages: extremely strong user motivation (expectation of making money), extremely high liquidity turnover (high-frequency trading), and extremely low barrier to entry (buying long or short). However, these advantages are also its ceiling. What are the bottlenecks of the casino model? Over a longer period, the casino model will gradually reveal several unavoidable problems: 1. Unsustainable user growth (reliant on the "newbie" model) The casino model is essentially PVP, not PVE. In other words: The profits of one group must be built on the losses of another. This directly leads to: User growth being highly dependent on "new funds" Once new user growth slows down, the system begins to involute; Retention is extremely low, with the vast majority of users participating only once; Ultimately, this forms a typical structure: "Traffic-driven → Bubble expansion → Liquidity depletion → Price collapse". 2. Inability to accumulate long-term value (no "productivity") The casino model creates almost no real value, merely transferring value. In contrast: Web2 creates information, content, and network effects; AI creates productivity and automation capabilities. Most crypto applications are merely "more efficient speculative tools." This leads to a core problem: capital can come in, but it won't stay long. 3. Regulatory pressure is inherent. When the core activity of an industry is: High-frequency trading, leverage, speculation, it will naturally be classified as a financial risk system, not a technological innovation system. This is also why globally: Exchanges are subject to the strictest regulation; Token issuance is constantly under scrutiny; DeFi has always been in a gray area; The more successful the casino model, the stronger the regulation. 4. Product Experience Fails to Expand its Reach Most "casino-style products" suffer from one problem: Strong Financial Attributes > High Risk Perception > High Learning Cost. For Web2 users, there's no need to take on complexity and risk for the sake of "potentially making money." Therefore, while the crypto industry talks about Mass Adoption, users remain primarily trading users, not "users." The real breakthrough: Shifting from a "financial system" to a "productivity system" If the crypto industry in the past solved the problem of "how to trade value more efficiently," then the next stage must solve the problem of "how to create new value." And this answer may very well come from AI × Blockchain. AI is essentially a productivity tool, while blockchain is essentially: [Identification of rights] [Settlement] [Verifiability] The core significance of combining the two lies in enabling both the "production process" and the "production result" to be recorded, verified, and assetized. This is a completely different path from the casino model. A Key Paradigm Shift: From "Trading Assets" to "Generating Assets" Past: Tokens were financing tools. NFTs were collectibles. User behavior = transactions. Future: Data is an asset. Models are assets. The behavior of AI agents is also an asset. User behavior will shift to a cycle of creation → use → reuse → profit, rather than simply buying and selling. Case Study: How 0G Labs Turns "Memory" into Assets Taking 0G as an example, we can see a very different path. A core module of 0G is decentralized storage + AI computing, which brings a crucial capability: the on-chain realization of AI Memory. What does this mean? In traditional AI systems: User data is stored on the platform; AI's "memory" is not transferable; all value belongs to the platform. However, in an architecture like 0G: User interaction data can be recorded on the blockchain; AI's memory can become a verifiable, ownable asset; different applications can share the same "memory layer." This directly brings several changes: 1. Memory as Asset

Your:

  • Usage Habits

  • Preferences

  • Data Trajectory

No longer just "data used by the platform," but an asset you can own, authorize, and even monetize.

2. AI Agents Possess "Inheritable State" Traditional AI: Each application is an island. Context is not transferable. On-chain, however, AI Agents can share memories across applications. This means that: AI is no longer a one-off tool, but rather a "continuously growing digital entity." 3. New business models are emerging. When "memory" can be assetized: Data markets, Agent economies, and Personalized AI services will all become true PVE (Value Creation) systems, not PVP (Value Validation). Conclusion: The essence of Mass Adoption is "De-Casinoization". For the crypto industry to truly move to the next stage, it must undergo a fundamental transformation: from asset issuance + transaction-driven to productivity-driven + value creation. In other words: De-Casinoization. This doesn't mean trading will disappear, but rather: Trading is no longer the core focus; speculation is no longer the sole entry point; users are no longer just "gamblers," but "creators." The previous generation of the crypto industry focused on making money flow; the next generation will focus on creating value. And AI may be the most important variable on this path.

data.title: Young people from small towns who label large AI models
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md

Datong, Shanxi, a city that once relied on coal for half of its economy, has now shaken off its coal dust, picked up a sharp pickaxe, and is smashing it down on another invisible mine.

In the office buildings of the Jinmao International Center in Pingcheng District, there are no more elevator shafts, no more coal trucks.

In the office buildings of the Jinmao International Center in Pingcheng District, there are no more elevator shafts, no more coal trucks.

Instead, thousands of tightly packed computer workstations have been replaced. The Shanghai Runxun Cloud Valley Big Data Smart Service Base occupies several floors, where thousands of young employees wearing headphones are staring at screens, clicking, dragging, and selecting. According to official data, as of November 2025, Datong City had 745,000 servers in operation, attracted 69 data labeling companies, created over 30,000 local jobs, and generated 750 million yuan in output value. In this digital mine, 94% of the workers are local residents. It's not just Datong. Among the first batch of data labeling bases designated by the National Data Administration, counties in central and western China, such as Yonghe County in Shanxi, Bijie in Guizhou, and Mengzi in Yunnan, are prominently featured. At the Yonghe County data labeling base, 80% of the employees are women. Most of them are stay-at-home mothers in rural areas, or young people who have returned to their hometowns because they couldn't find suitable jobs. A hundred years ago, the textile factories of Manchester, England, were crowded with farmers who had lost their land. Today, in front of computer screens in these remote county towns, sit young people who can't find their place in the real economy. They are engaged in a futuristic yet extremely primitive piecework, producing the data feed necessary for large models for artificial intelligence giants in Beijing, Shenzhen, and Silicon Valley. No one sees anything wrong with this. The essence of data annotation is teaching machines to recognize the world. Autonomous driving requires recognizing traffic lights and pedestrians, while large models need to distinguish between cats and dogs. Machines themselves lack common sense; humans must first draw a box on an image to show it "this is a pedestrian" before it can learn to recognize things on its own after absorbing millions of images. This job doesn't require a high level of education, only patience and a constantly clicking index finger. In the golden age of 2017, a simple 2D box could cost over ten cents, with some companies even offering as much as fifty cents. Fast-handed annotators could work over ten hours a day and earn five or six hundred yuan. In a county town, this was definitely a high-paying and respectable job. However, as large models evolved, the harsh reality of this production line began to emerge. By 2023, the unit price for simple image annotation had plummeted to 3 to 4 cents, a drop of over 90%. Even for more complex 3D point cloud maps—images composed of dense points that require magnification countless times to see their edges—annotators had to create a 3D bounding box with length, width, height, and tilt angles to perfectly enclose vehicles or pedestrians. Such a complex 3D bounding box only cost 5 cents. The direct consequence of the plummeting unit price is a dramatic increase in labor intensity. To cling to their meager monthly base salary of two to three thousand yuan, annotators must constantly and relentlessly improve their typing speed. This is by no means an easy white-collar job. In many annotation bases, management is suffocatingly strict; phone calls are not allowed during work hours, and mobile phones must be locked in storage compartments. The system precisely records each employee's mouse movement and dwell time. If a pause exceeds three minutes, a warning from the backend will lash out like a whip. Even more frustrating is the error tolerance. The industry standard is typically above 95%, with some companies even requiring 98%-99%. This means that if you draw 100 boxes and only two are wrong, the entire drawing will be sent back for revision. Dynamic drawings are frame-by-frame; vehicles changing lanes can be obscured, and annotators must rely on association to find them one by one. In 3D point cloud maps, any object with more than 10 points must be bounded. In a complex parking space project, lines that are too long or missing markings will always be found fault during quality control. Revising a drawing four or five times is commonplace. In the end, after spending an hour, you only get a few cents. A data annotator in Hunan posted her settlement statement on social media. After a day's work, she drew over 700 boxes, each worth 4 cents, for a total income of 30.2 yuan. This presents a starkly contrasting picture. On one hand, there are glamorous tech leaders at press conferences, discussing how AGI will liberate humanity; on the other hand, in county towns on the Loess Plateau and in the mountains of Southwest China, young people stare at screens for eight to ten hours a day, mechanically drawing thousands, tens of thousands of boxes, even dreaming of lane lines in the air. Someone once said that artificial intelligence appears as a luxury car speeding by, but if you open the door, you'll find a hundred people riding bicycles, gritting their teeth and pedaling furiously. Nobody sees anything wrong with that. The pieceworkers teaching machines "how to love" Once the bottleneck in image recognition was broken, large-scale models underwent a deeper evolution; they needed to learn to think, converse, and even demonstrate "empathy" like humans. This gave rise to the most crucial and expensive aspect of large-scale model training—RLHF (Reinforcement Learning Based on Human Feedback). In simple terms, it involves having real people rate the AI-generated answers, telling it which answers are better and more in line with human values ​​and emotional preferences. ChatGPT appears "human-like" because it has countless RLHF (Reference Language Learning and Highly Formal) annotators training it. On crowdsourcing platforms, these annotation tasks are often openly priced: 3 to 7 yuan per task. Annotators need to give highly subjective emotional scores to the AI's answers, judging whether the answer is "warm," "empathetic," and "considerate of the user's emotions." A low-level worker earning a monthly salary of two or three thousand, struggling in the mire of reality, and having no time to even care about their own emotions, is forced to act as an emotional mentor and value judge for AI within the system. They need to forcibly break down and quantify extremely complex and subtle human emotions such as warmth and empathy into cold scores of 1 to 5. If their scores don't match the system's standard answers, they're judged as having a low accuracy rate, resulting in a deduction from their already meager piece-rate wages. This is a form of cognitive vacuum. Humanity's complex and subtle emotions, morality, and compassion are being forcibly dragged into the funnel of algorithms. In the cold scale of quantification and standardization, they are drained of their last bit of warmth. While you marvel at the cyber behemoths on your screen that have learned to write poetry and compose music, to offer comfort, and even don a melancholic facade, outside the screen, those once vibrant humans are degenerating into emotionless scoring machines through day after day of mechanical judgment. This is the most hidden side of the entire industry chain, never appearing in any financing news or technology white papers. Nobody sees anything wrong with it.

985 Master's Degree and Small-Town Youth

The basic task of creating frameworks is being crushed by the conveyor belt of AI. This cyber assembly line is beginning to spread upwards, devouring higher-level intellectual labor.

The appetite of large models has changed. It is no longer satisfied with chewing up simple common sense; it needs to devour human professional knowledge and high-level logic.

A special type of part-time job is frequently appearing on major recruitment platforms, such as "Large Model Logical Reasoning Labeling" and "AI Humanities Trainer". The threshold for this part-time job is extremely high, often requiring "a master's degree or above from a 985/211 university", involving professional fields such as law, medicine, philosophy, and literature.

Many graduate students from prestigious universities are attracted to these large companies' outsourcing groups. However, they quickly discover that this is not an easy mental workout, but rather a form of mental torture. Before officially accepting orders, they must read dozens of pages of scoring dimensions and evaluation criteria documents and conduct two to three rounds of trial annotations. After meeting the standards, if their accuracy rate is below average in the official annotation process, they will be disqualified and kicked out of the group chat. What's most suffocating is that these standards aren't fixed at all. Faced with similar questions and answers, using the same way of thinking to score them can yield completely opposite results. It's like taking an exam that's never finished and has no standard answer. Unable to improve accuracy through self-effort or learning, you can only keep going in circles, wasting mental and physical energy. This is the new form of exploitation in the era of big models—class folding. Knowledge, once seen as the golden ladder to break down barriers and climb upwards, has now become digital fodder offered to algorithms, more complex to chew on. Faced with the absolute power of algorithms and systems, the ivory tower master's students from top universities and the small-town youths from the Loess Plateau meet with the most bizarre convergence. They all fell into this bottomless cyber mine, stripped of their halos, their differences erased, all reduced to cheap, replaceable gears on the tracks. The same is true abroad. In 2024, Apple directly cut a 121-person AI voice annotation team in San Diego. These employees were responsible for improving Siri's multilingual processing capabilities; they once thought they were on the periphery of a major company's core business, but instantly plunged into the abyss of unemployment. In the eyes of tech giants, whether it's a small-town auntie pulling boxes or a logic trainer graduated from a prestigious university, they are essentially replaceable "consumables." Nobody sees anything wrong with this. The Tower of Babel, worth trillions, is built with pennies of blood and sweat. According to data released by the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology (CAICT), the Chinese data labeling market reached 6.08 billion yuan in 2023 and is projected to reach 20-30 billion yuan in 2025. It is predicted that by 2030, the global data labeling and service market will surge to 117.1 billion yuan. Behind these figures lies the valuation frenzy of tech giants like OpenAI, Microsoft, and ByteDance, with valuations reaching hundreds of billions or even trillions of dollars. However, this immense wealth has not flowed to those who truly "feed" AI. China's data annotation industry exhibits a typical inverted pyramid outsourcing structure. At the top are the tech giants who tightly control the core algorithms; the second layer consists of large data service providers; the third layer comprises data annotation bases and small-to-medium-sized outsourcing companies scattered across the country; and at the bottom are the labor-intensive annotation workers paid by piecework. Each layer of outsourcing takes a cut. When a large company offers 50 cents per unit, after layers of skimming, the annotation worker in a county town might receive less than 5 cents. Former Greek Finance Minister Yannis Varoufakis, in his book *Technological Feudalism*, put forward a highly insightful view: today's tech giants are no longer capitalists in the traditional sense, but rather "cloud lords." What they possess are not factories and machines, but algorithms, platforms, and computing power—the digital territory of the cyber age. In this new feudal system, users are not consumers, but digital tenants. Every like, comment, and view we make on social media is providing data to the cloud lords for free. And those data labelers distributed in lower-tier markets are the lowest-level digital serfs in this system. They not only produce data, but also clean, classify, and score massive amounts of raw data, transforming it into high-quality feed that large models can digest. This is a covert cognitive land grab. Just as the Enclosure Movement in 19th-century England drove farmers into textile factories, today's AI wave is driving young people who cannot find their place in the real economy to the screen. AI hasn't bridged the class divide; instead, it's created a "data and sweatshop transport belt" stretching from county towns in central and western China to the headquarters of tech giants in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen. The narrative of technological revolution is always grand and glamorous, but its underlying reality is always the large-scale consumption of cheap labor. Nobody sees anything wrong with this. The cruelest ending is fast approaching, and faster than ever. With the leap in the capabilities of large-scale models, labeling tasks that once required round-the-clock human labor are being taken over by AI itself. In April 2023, Li Xiang, founder of Li Auto, revealed data at a forum showing that in the past, Li Auto had to manually calibrate approximately 10 million frames of autonomous driving images annually, with outsourcing costs approaching 100 million yuan. However, after using large models for automated annotation, what used to take a year could be completed in approximately 3 hours. This efficiency is 1000 times that of humans, and this was back in 2023. Just last March, Li Auto also released its new generation MindVLA-o1 automatic annotation engine. A self-deprecating saying circulates in the industry: "The more intelligence, the more manual labor." But now, major companies' investment in data annotation outsourcing has seen a precipitous drop of 40%-50%. Those young people from small towns who spent countless days and nights sitting in front of their computers, their eyes bloodshot, fed a behemoth with their own hands. And now, this behemoth is turning around and smashing their rice bowls. Night falls, and the office buildings in Pingcheng District of Datong remain as white as day. Young people changing shifts silently exchange weary bodies in the elevators. In this folded space tightly bound by countless polygonal frames, no one cares about the epic leap the Transformer architecture across the ocean has achieved, nor does anyone understand the roar of computing power behind hundreds of billions of parameters. Their gaze was fixed solely on the red and green progress bar in the background, representing the "passing grade," calculating whether those few cents or dimes of piecework could piece together a decent life by the end of the month. On one side, the Nasdaq bell rang and tech media relentlessly reported on AGI's arrival; on the other, these digital serfs, feeding AI with their own flesh and blood, could only wait anxiously in their aching sleep for the beast they had personally nurtured to carelessly kick their rice bowls away on some seemingly ordinary morning. No one saw anything wrong with this.

data.title: Polymarket Ditches Risky USDC.e for Native Stablecoin in Its Biggest Upgrade Ever
data.description:
Polymarket, the largest fully onchain prediction market, is overhauling its exchange infrastructure, replacing bridged USDC.e with a native USDC-backed token, revamping its trading engine, and rolling out multisig wallet support—marking the platform’s boldest move since launch.

We've heard your feedback, and we're excited to announce Polymarket is getting a full exchange upgrade.

Over the next few weeks, we're rolling out a rebuilt trading engine, upgraded smart contracts, and a new collateral token (Polymarket USD) to move off USDC.e. 🧵

— Polymarket (@Polymarket) April 6, 2026

At the center of Polymarket’s upgrade: Polymarket USD, a 1:1 USDC-backed native token replacing USDC.e, the bridged Polygon version. By moving away from cross-chain collateral, Polymarket eliminates settlement vulnerabilities while keeping dollar parity.

Polymarket is Launching Their Own Collateral Token: Polymarket USD!@Polymarket just dropped a big update. They’re moving away from USDC.e and bringing in their own collateral token, Polymarket USD, backed 1:1 by USDC.

Rolling out over the next 2-3 weeks.

For most users,… pic.twitter.com/0GrJoXRGow

— Pranjal Bora 🧭 (@Crypto_Pranjal) April 6, 2026

“For most users, conversion is seamless with a one-time frontend approval,” Polymarket said, noting power users can manually wrap via the Collateral Onramp contract. This shift dramatically reduces counterparty risk, enhances transparency, and aligns with tightening U.S. regulatory standards.

Trading Engine Gets Full Rebuild

Polymarket is deploying version 2 exchange contracts, its most significant infrastructure change since 2020. With V2, Polymarket's faster and more efficient matching engine requires fewer operations and lower gas fees.

V2 will also feature an updated Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) combining onchain execution with offchain efficiency.

"During the upgrade, all existing order books will be cleared. There will be a short maintenance window. We'll announce the exact date and time at least one week in advance."

One of the most significant upgrades is Polymarket’s support for EIP-1271, enabling multisig and smart contract wallets to access institutional-grade trading tools that were previously restricted to traditional wallets.

EIP-1271 support signals institutional appetite.

Multisigs, DAOs, and fund vehicles use contract wallets.

Adding 1271 support means Polymarket is either seeing demand from or actively courting institutional/treasury participants who can't use EOA hot wallets for compliance… https://t.co/QMhFRHxHxN

— Arjun Kalsy (@ArjunKalsy) April 6, 2026

Developers integrating bots or custom SDKs will need to update orders, while most users experience minimal disruption during a brief maintenance window.

This technical overhaul coincides with Polymarket’s CFTC approval to operate an intermediated trading platform in the United States, enabling direct onboarding of brokers and customers through regulated venues. The move signals a compliance-first strategy, positioning Polymarket as the U.S.’s premier regulated prediction market.

Backing from NYSE parent Intercontinental Exchange ($600M investment) underscores institutional confidence and highlights the startup’s pivot toward mainstream adoption.

Explosive growth and institutional ambitions

Interest in prediction markets—covering politics, markets, and policy—has continue to grow, with March volumes hitting $10.4 billion, boosted by expanded trading fees.

V2’s developer-friendly contracts and native USDC make Polymarket an API layer for quant funds, automated strategies, and multisig wallets, previously excluded by technical limitations.

Polymarket’s bold upgrades trade bridged token risk for regulatory alignment, transforming the platform from a crypto-centric prediction market into a compliant, institutional-grade oracle infrastructure ready for 2026 and beyond.

data.title: South Korea Orders Monthly Audits And Real-Time Balance Checks For All Crypto Exchanges Following $56 Billion Bitcoin Error
data.description:
South Korea Orders Crypto Exchanges To Verify User Assets Every Five Minutes

South Korea’s financial authorities have imposed strict new rules on cryptocurrency exchanges, requiring all platforms to reconcile their user asset balances against actual holdings every five minutes.

The Financial Services Commission (FSC) announced the measures as part of a broader system improvement plan aimed at preventing operational failures that could undermine market trust.

S.Korea's FSC tightens crypto exchange oversight after Bithumb error. Exchanges must reconcile ledgers every 5 mins, disclose balances daily & undergo monthly audits by end of May. Stricter regs ahead. #crypto#S Korea #regulation

— Blockchain Report (@blockchain24R) April 6, 2026

Exchanges must also implement automatic trading halt mechanisms to stop transactions if discrepancies exceed set thresholds, alongside monthly external audits and expanded disclosure of holdings.

Why Did The Regulator Act So Quickly

The move follows a severe operational mishap at Bithumb in February, where the exchange mistakenly credited roughly 620,000 BTC, valued at around $56 billion at the time, to 249 users during a promotional event.

Each recipient received what was intended to be 2,000 Korean won but instead received thousands of dollars’ worth of Bitcoin.

Bithumb accidentally distributed 2,000 BTC to users instead of $1.50 rewards 🚨

Here’s what actually happened:

On February 6, 2026, around 10:30 UTC, Bithumb ran a “Random Box” promotional event.

The reward was supposed to be 2,000 KRW.

(Around $1.50)

Instead, an internal… pic.twitter.com/I3csOUUngv

— StarPlatinum (@StarPlatinum_) February 6, 2026

Some users sold the mistakenly issued coins immediately, causing a temporary 10–17% drop in the token’s price on the platform.

While Bithumb froze the affected accounts and recovered most of the assets, the incident exposed deep flaws in internal control systems across the sector.

What Exchanges Need To Change

The FSC’s new framework addresses these vulnerabilities directly.

All exchanges must now reconcile their internal ledgers with on-chain holdings at five-minute intervals, a dramatic increase from the daily or occasional checks previously in place.

Automatic alerts and kill switches must trigger immediately if mismatches surpass predefined limits.

Monthly independent audits replace the previous quarterly schedule, and public disclosures must now include detailed blockchain holdings for each type of virtual asset rather than a simple coverage ratio.

Manual, high-risk transactions such as event rewards must be executed from separate accounts, with validity-check systems that automatically block irregular inputs.

A third-party cross-verification is required before any payment is confirmed.

Most exchanges currently allow transactions to proceed with approval from a single manager, a practice the FSC has identified as a major risk.

Structural Weaknesses Revealed

Emergency inspections carried out after the Bithumb incident found that three of the five major exchanges only reconciled balances every 24 hours, while most lacked multi-step approval systems for high-risk transactions.

Proprietary and high-risk accounts were often combined, and automated verification of payment plans against actual recipients was missing.

Shin Jin-chang, Secretary General of the FSC, said during a meeting with exchange representatives and the Digital Asset Exchange Alliance (DAXA) on 6 April,

“The emergency inspection following the Bithumb Bitcoin mis-payment incident revealed not only the human error cited as the superficial cause but also structural and customary problems that have accumulated at the exchanges.”

🚨 CRYPTO: SOUTH KOREA ORDERS ALL EXCHANGES TO VERIFY HOLDINGS EVERY 5 MINUTES AFTER BITHUMB'S $56B BITCOIN BLUNDER

South Korea's Financial Services Commission is mandating that all crypto exchanges build always-on balance reconciliation systems operating on 5-minute cycles. The… pic.twitter.com/C5JIsNx7P6

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 6, 2026

Regulatory Oversight And Industry Response

The FSC will strengthen internal controls to align with standards expected of traditional financial companies.

Compliance monitoring will move from annual to semi-annual inspections, with mandatory reporting to financial authorities.

Exchanges are also required to appoint dedicated risk management officers and form Risk Management Committees.

DAXA will finalise self-regulatory rule revisions by the end of April, with IT systems supporting constant balance reconciliation expected to be operational by May.

Key aspects of the reforms are expected to feed into South Korea’s forthcoming second-phase Digital Asset Act, giving regulators legal authority to impose business suspensions, personnel sanctions, and administrative fines for violations.

These measures aim to reduce operational risks and ensure that high-value transactions can be executed safely without relying solely on human oversight.

This regulatory overhaul represents one of the first cases where a major financial regulator has applied near real-time internal audit requirements directly to the cryptocurrency sector, targeting operational weaknesses rather than cyberattacks.

data.title: Who is the first US-based large-scale model stock?
data.description:
At an AI summit, Ultraman and Amodei refused to join hands

Author: Su Yang, Tencent Technology

The IPO race between OpenAI and Anthropic is one of the most watched IPO competitions in Silicon Valley.

The IPO race between OpenAI and Anthropic is one of the most watched IPO races in Silicon Valley.

Neither company wants to fall behind the other and both hope to complete their IPOs by the end of 2026. However, behind the competition for the title of "first large-scale model stock," their financial situations and internal pacing differ significantly. OpenAI CEO Altman wants to go public as soon as possible, but his CFO believes the company isn't ready yet. Anthropic is experiencing rapid revenue growth but also faces enormous computational cost pressures. Both companies rely on massive investments in computing power to maintain competitiveness, and the return on these investments is uncertain.
